title: Support (m)TLS API Socket
body:
Add flags `--tls-cert`, `--tls-key`, `--tls-ca`/`--tls-client-ca` to the commands `podman remote`, `podman system service`, and `podman system connection add` to support serving the API socket using TLS and mTLS, as well as connecting to such a socket.

This relies on https://github.com/containers/common/pull/2249 and will fail CI until merged.

Fixes https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/24583

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
* The `podman system service` command now supports serving over tcp with TLS and mTLS
* The `podman system connection add` command now supports creating connections to TLS and mTLS tcp sockets
* The `podman remote` commands now support connecting to TLS and mTLS tcp sockets
```

@jwhonce @baude @mheon FYI. Had you ever looked at TLS support for the podman service and remote client?
With the amount of code here this looks easy enough to accept to me but I was wondering if there is/was any reason why we do not support it.

I don’t think we ever looked into this. Largely because without certificate
authentication - which is hard to do - TLS doesn’t add much. Other people
not being any to read the traffic didn’t matter if we are exposing
passwordless root over the network.

It is not being send anywhere, both the client and server read the files locally and then use the certificates to perform a normal TLS handshake via the go std lib AFAICT.

> @Luap99 I seem to remember the idea behind using the ssh executable as well as the golang libraries was to push this kind of handshake into that code vs. podman. But @cdoern did that work, so I don't know where it stands.

> Also this will need some e2e or system tests where we spawn a server with TLS and the connect with the remove client.

Of course. I was hoping to have my environment capable of passing tests on main before I went on vacation last week, but that didn't end up happening. After throwing up my hands and just making a fresh CentOS VM, I have that working now. 

I'd like to use any existing tests for SSH/TCP remotes as a basis for my tests, as well as ensure my tests have parity with them, but for the life of me, I cannot seem to find any such tests. Would you mind pointing me to where they are?

@Luap99 sorry, just back to this.  This line: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/24601/files#diff-cf5cc76e70a369c5221f08f29ec0452710af1f4ecd423c162d5b669688416caeR525  seemed to indicate that you were looking for the directory where the private key resigned.  Unless I misinterpreted, that doesn't feel right.

> @Luap99 sorry, just back to this. This line: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/24601/files#diff-cf5cc76e70a369c5221f08f29ec0452710af1f4ecd423c162d5b669688416caeR525 seemed to indicate that you were looking for the directory where the private key resigned. Unless I misinterpreted, that doesn't feel right.

Yes, the purpose of this line is to create a command line argument so that the user can provide the path to their TLS client private key, which is necessary to perform authentication. Unless I too have misinterpreted something else in the codebase, neither that path, nor especially not the data in that file, ever goes over the wire. This is the analog of the `--identity` flag for SSH authentication.

> Also this will need some e2e or system tests where we spawn a server with TLS and the connect with the remove client.

Apologies for the delay on this, a number of other items were pushed onto my priority stack, but I've been able to resume work on it.

As mentioned, there weren't any substantial e2e tests for the TCP API, and the libpod remote test suite was entirely using the unix socket, so I have refactored it to allow running it for unix, plain TCP, TLS, and mTLS. This might seem excessive, but it was only by running this complete suite did I find additional issues I had to fix. Suggestions are welcome for how to achieve similar coverage with fewer tests.

Speaking of said issues, it would seem that the container attach client has a subtle race condition in it, as well as a memory leak.

By overwriting the http transport and capturing the net.Conn as a local variable, if the same Connection is used concurrently, there is a possibility that the first net.Conn will be overwritten on the first attach call's local variable, resulting in the two calls proxying each-other's streams, or just outright corrupting them.

As well, because the original transport is never restored, if the same client is used to attach to multiple containers, the closure is never freed, meaning the dial function will call an ever-increasing stack of closures. Fixing this issue felt outside the scope of this PR, but I have added a note warning of it to whoever feels like addressing it in the future.

I would suggest adding some sort of `DoRequestWithConn` method which handles using the new transport for that request alone and manually performs the dialing, writing the request, and reading the response, and validating the upgrade.

Finally, there were a number of other e2e tests that were failing in my fresh centos 9 environment, even on main, so I have simply ignored them for the time being.

This feature has become a priority for us, so I'm going to try to push this over the finish line, working on top of this draft. @meln5674 If you have any concerns, please feel free to reach out and let me know :blush:

The feature is basically done, and I'm happy to finish it myself, but I've been waiting for a second review since February. Its only marked as draft as it depends on the other PR in common to be merged first.
Will do. I'll need to spend a bit of time resurrecting the dev VM I had for this today to make sure tests still all pass locally after rebasing.

Less progress than I would have liked today, had to spend time putting my homelab virtualization cluster back together after it had been scavenged for parts over the intervening months. Rebased both podman and common, and added the go.mod replacement and re-vendored to get CI going.

Unit tests are passing locally. Remote E2E tests for the ssh/unix transport are flaking, really badly, as in, "can't get a successful run" kind of badly. This is odd, since that wasn't the subject of any of my changes, and the TCP/TLS/mTLS E2E tests work fine, which are all the same tests, just over a different transport. Errors are consistently about permissions issue of a `layer.lock` file. Not sure if this an issue resulting from changes on main, or something else I have overlooked. 

There seems to have been a test added in the last 6 months that doesn't make sense within the idea of multiple remote transports. I have disabled it temporarily in order to get CI going.

Tomorrow, or failing that, over the weekend, I hope to integrate that new test with the tests for multiple transports, as well as add a few last tests in order to confirm that every combination of getting the tls material from connection/flags/env/none works as expected, and finally address any CI findings.

@ashley-cui It appears the CI is pedantic enough that each individual commit has to pass, so I did a history rewrite to put the common fork switch as the first commit. Even after doing so appears to trigger some sort of ["bloat detection"](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5398950124978176?logs=main#L231) on that commit, which proceeds to kill the rest of the CI jobs. Please advise.
Bloat approved tag added, CI restarted

Sorry for the radio silence on this. In addition to some 9-to-5 stuff preempting this, I was seeing a failure on [this system test](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/test/system/090-events.bats#L32) and, despite it being unrelated to this PR, was convinced it was an issue on my end. Not sure why it didn't occur to me to check this sooner, but its also failing on main (c73712b66337ff0d954d4acd196da7bc42da4a65) on a VM from a [fedora cloud image](https://fedoraproject.org/cloud/download) from a week ago.

For the time being, I rebased to fix the conflicts and fixed the other failing system test you noted above to see if it will also fail in CI.

It doesn't look like the failure I'm seeing locally is reflected in CI, thankfully, but the two new failures are both external:

`couldn't search registry "docker.io": Requesting bearer token: received unexpected HTTP status: 400 Bad Request`

I'd suggest moving any tests like this that rely on external services to a separate suite, and run them as a separate job with a high retry count and a long delay between retries, or some sort of exponential backoff, in order to avoid any spurious failures based on circumstances outside of developer's control.

The last thing I'd like to do is add the system tests that verify the combinations of flags, connections, and env vars for TLS options, but I'm unsure of you would like me go about doing that, as it touches on the same issues as the integration tests.

It looks like the current `make remotesystem` is hard-coded to spin up a podman service over a unix socket. Given that these new tests I want to add would be only valid for some remotes (emphasis on plural), should I:
* Make a separate make target that just verifies the different remotes?
* Update this target to run the suite multiple times with different podman services?
* Use make vars to change the behavior of the single target to run different remotes and enable only the valid tests, relying on the CI matrix for coverage?
* Something else I haven't considered?

Also, for maintainability, I'd strongly suggest moving the tangle of backslashes for that remotesystem target into a dedicated script file.

@ashley-cui 
> I think its sufficient just to test that mTLS/TLS works, and fails when an incorrect cert is given or a key is missing.

When I started working on this, I would have agreed with you, but there is an unfortunate wrinkle that necessitates at least *some* granular testing of tls/mtls.

If the only change were to how the connection was initiated, then a simple smoke test like you're describing would be adequate. However, [these](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/24601/files/c73712b66337ff0d954d4acd196da7bc42da4a65#diff-02a7031ca65fa34d69f3de533b25111aa07a03242c86c4660dbd35ec69d1cd2fR136) two [sections](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/24601/files/c73712b66337ff0d954d4acd196da7bc42da4a65#diff-02a7031ca65fa34d69f3de533b25111aa07a03242c86c4660dbd35ec69d1cd2fR508) had to be added in order to make the "streaming" functionality work. This is a result of using a non-standard approach to perform the HTTP connection upgrade to the "raw" protocol originally invented by docker (similar concept to websockets for anyone whom that's a more familiar topic).

What this means is, there could be functionality that is silently broken that would not be surfaced until someone attempted to use *every* piece of functionality over a TLS connection. If new functionality were to be added in the future that also leveraged this connection upgrade, that functionality could be released in a broken state for TLS connection, without noticing, as it would never be executed over TLS during CI.

I myself made this mistake, as I only had one of those two sections added, and was unaware that the other was required until I ran the full integration suite against a TLS remote. If I can make that mistake when I'm specifically working on TLS, what chance does someone who might not even know that TLS functionality even exists?

I agree that the method I have so far is sub-optimal, and needs to be reworked, but I strongly believe that the other connection methods need to have at least *some* robust testing in CI beyond '`podman ps` works, ship it'.

Having slept on the problem, I want to take a step back and ensure there aren't any conflicting unspoken assumptions. To be explicit, my goals with the tests the way I have/was planning on doing them is to prove the following:

1. Existing unix/ssh remote functionality works as it did before
2. Desired properties of (m)TLS hold (client rejects untrusted server, server rejects untrusted server, neither attempt to run with missing/malformed material)
3. Existing functionality (running containers, streaming logs, etc) works over the new transports

The way I intended to prove those were:

1. Existing tests execute without modification
2. Tests which contrive scenarios where those rejections occurs (bogus cert, garbage data in cert file, outright missing, etc)
3. Run a small selection of existing test cases against the new transports (e.g. run a container, run with attached streams, stream logs, pull an image, etc)

You've made mention of the tests in point 2, so it appears we agree on that. Can you please clarify if the disagreement for point 3 is purely in implementation, or do you disagree that it needs to be proven at all?

@meln5674 Thanks for the clarification! 3 is mostly on implementation. The labels solution is just too difficult to maintain, and also makes it difficult to understand why that test is failing and if it's run with a different transport. Beyond that, I think it's a good idea to test some core podman functionality with TLS, something like testing image pull, running a container, doing a build, and a few more test cases where you see fit might be okay. Running the entire suite with TLS seems to be too much to me, I think it's enough to trust our split testing there. If bugs arise, we can always fix them, and then add a specific test case for that bug as they are fixed.

That sounds reasonable then. I think then what makes sense is for the remote suites to test:
1. All transport-agnostic functionality against *a* transport (configurable w/ a Makefile variable)
2. All transport-specific functionality against the matching transport
3. Spot check basic functionality against each transport

For point 1, the goal is to allow developers to the full suite against a transport they are working on, but the default would be run as it is today. It will also weed out any tests that are in 1 but belong in 2.

Does this sound acceptable?

@meln5674 Yes, mostly sounds good to me! 

1. The only thing I would change is to use an env var instead of a separate makefile target, so something like `REMOTE_TRANSPORT=tls make localintegration` would run the integration tests using tls. 
2. I would put these in a system test, similar to the CORS tests, using `$PODMAN system service --tls-cert ...`
3. We can add these to the system tests in 2 by hiting the server using `$PODMAN --remote --tls cert.....` etc. A weird gotcha would be that this would be a local test only, as technically it tests the local service, but I think that's easy enough to understand after a second look.

Let me know what you think!

> I think they're okay as-is. It makes sense to me that we're testing the remote connection, so in the test we spin up a special service to test the remote connection. I feel like running it again as a per-remote connection might be too much

To be clear, what I'm suggesting is that, given there are already tests that say "spin up a {unix,tcp} service, validate you can use it via system connection add", that I should
A. Duplicate those tests for tls and mtls to confirm the system connections work for the other two transports.
B. Create similar tests for each of the 4 transports that a service can be accessed just using the --host flag or CONTAINER_HOST var instead of a system connection, totally independent of the main service spun up in the makefile.
C. Add at least one check to each of the above that verifies the raw tcp upgrade (i.e. logs -f or exec -t)

This would confirm that each of the remote connections pass the "smell test", without having to run the full suite of tests against them.

What I was saying in regards to "treating them differently", would be to skip them during the first part of the remotesystem test while the global podman service is running, then run them separately after it had been spun down. If that's too much complexity and the overhead of a second podman service is acceptable, then I have no issues with doing it as its done now.

@meln5674 Thanks for the clarification, I think honestly it's too much complexity, and it's best to just leave it as it is right now. This is already excellent test coverage. We can always add more tests later as issues pop up and we resolve them.

@ashley-cui 
I have added the tests I described to verify the different remotes and their options, and I'm glad I did, as they uncovered a few more bugs of mine I hadn't noticed, including a panic. I have also re-done the test certificate generation as a single target calling a script. I am tracking the two outstanding requests regarding moving the env vars to common and the --format=tls option, but I'd like a thumbs-up on the testing situation before I move onto that. I also noticed that my autoformatted somehow reactivated, so I've given up, and I will just revert the autoformatting at the end when I squash.
FYI we migrated c/common into a monorepo today - you need to move your c/common PR to c/container-libs, apologies for the inconvenience.
It looks like go.mod still points to common instead of container-libs, is there an expected timeline for the switch? How would you like me to proceed until then?

> It looks like go.mod still points to common instead of container-libs, is there an expected timeline for the switch?

That happened just now, in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26858 .

Testing-wise: I wonder if it wouldn't be easiest to have fedora N be non-TLS, and fedora N-1 be TLS, for the E2E tests (system tests look pretty reasonable, running the TLS bits separate seems fine there). We've done a split across OSes before (OCI runtime testing, for example) and it has worked well - Fedora N vs N-1 differences are generally small enough that we can get away with it.

> Testing-wise: I wonder if it wouldn't be easiest to have fedora N be non-TLS, and fedora N-1 be TLS, for the E2E tests (system tests look pretty reasonable, running the TLS bits separate seems fine there). We've done a split across OSes before (OCI runtime testing, for example) and it has worked well - Fedora N vs N-1 differences are generally small enough that we can get away with it.

@mheon Do you think that it's necessary to run our entire test suite with tls? I honestly think its suffiencient to just run a few system tests under tls. Maybe if we do decide to do split testing, it can be addressed in a separate PR.
@meln5674  Friendly ping, still working on this?
I can see an argument for doing a full-suite run in addition to the standard run, but I wouldn't call it a requirement to merge. I'd be comfortable with just system tests as well.

@ashley-cui I was waiting to hear back re: the env vars conversation above, and then some personal stuff came up. I should be able to resume within the next few days. I have mtrmac's feedback implemented, however, I wanted to get a chance to discuss the env vars again before I committed and rebased to the container-libs switch.

I still strongly believe that envs should be an option. As I mentioned, many tools in the space do this, such as the KUBECONFIG variable in kubectl and helm, the DOCKER_CERT_PATH for docker, CONTAINERD_ADDRESS for containerd, and so on. I'm still not sure I understand the motivation behind removing them is, and if it comes down to shaving a few seconds off the system tests, that feels like a poor tradeoff. Given that I'm already running into frustrations doing simple things as part of the system tests, I imagine anyone trying to use it for real would find the flags as the only override options to be an obstacle.

Unfortunately, its the system tests that are the issue, integration is running fine, and it really comes down to the decision to write the system tests in bash. Before, I could export the appropriate envs from the makefile helper script, and for tests that needed to use their own, it would unset them at the top of that test. I can no longer do that, so I have to invent new variables, and build the argument lists in a helper function, or use global mutable arrays, except some tests don't use the helper functions, or use them in a way that conflicts with how other tests expect to use them, or some tests use subshells which don't get arrays, so I have to go through and fix or fudge a decent chunk of the other tests that have nothing to do with this feature. Its not that its a difficult engineering challenge, its just tedious due to how its written.

I should have time later today to pick this up and report back with specifics. Can you elaborate on the deadline?

Umm. We have done environment variables for testing only for other features (user namespaces, for example)... I wouldn't want them documented, but maybe testing-only ways of setting up TLS via environment is not the worst idea?

> I wouldn't want them documented, but maybe testing-only ways of setting up TLS via environment is not the worst idea?

A somewhat weak opinion: A “testing-only undocumented” way to affect security settings instinctively makes me worried about unexpected / hard-to-audit ways to affect configuration. (It would be plausible to have testing-only ways to affect variables _that are only possible in testing builds_, but then we would not be testing exactly the production code.)
Eugh. I've always maintained the position that if you have to put undocumented things in production code to facilitate testing, you don't have a testable architecture.

https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/24601/files#diff-97ca1f56a5c2144f31838c7a840926d72dc582cfbec5003e7588c4090cd30d90R36 is a prime example of what I'm running into. What should the `run_podman` helper function do? Should it add the flags? Always? Based on a test-only env like `REMOTESYSTEM_TLS_CA_CRT`? Not at all?

If it adds them always, the test breaks.
If it never adds them, every test has to add them manually.
If it adds them only when the test vars are set, you can unset them in the test, but now you have to duplicate helper functions to add those flags, since you can't encapsulate it into a function, because bash functions can't return arrays, only strings. Maybe I could write some sort of setup to marshal and unmarshal those arrays to strings, or throw caution to the wind and not care about the possibility of special characters.

Meanwhile, as you can see in its current state, when there are environment variables, I simply unexport them at the start of the test, and it works as expected.

@meln5674 Could we just start with doing the [simple spot check tls tests](https://github.com/ashley-cui/podman/commit/7d68c6066ee4e5e3b4b1011d97a2b950a9321d0e), and then do the makefile/manual test target for then e2e/system tests in a followup PR? It looks testing this still has a lot of discussion that frankly should have been done in a design document. As mentioned, we have an approaching deadline of on our part, which is the end of the month. I'd hate to delay this feature going in, since it's complete, and the bulk of the dialogue is around testing. 

For the followup on extended testing, we should add flags on setup based on a testing env var. The tests that rely on tcp should get a skip_if_tls function, and we should just skip them, instead of forcing another protocol. This also makes sense - we should not be switching the protocol secretly behind the hood anyway.

I think I just needed some time away from the problem and a rubber duck, as after making that post an idea occurred to me of how to make this work using some more exotic bash features. I was able to get both unix and TCP remotesystem suites passing last night, and will make sure TLS and mTLS are passing this evening.

I will strive to rebase and have something for review by tomorrow morning (USA timezones). If there are no other changes requested beyond what's been commented so far, and I can get a clean CI run and pre-squash LGTM after that sometime tomorrow, I will try to get all of the final squash and cleanup done before the end of tomorrow.
It looks like the two CI failures were flakes/timeouts and unrelated to this PR, and pass locally, could someone please restart them?
Tests green
@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL
CI seems to be unhappy that boltdb is deprecated, but still trying to run jobs with it? Also SELinux issues, which I am wholly unqualified to diagnose.
Please squash the commits properly and remove the formatting changes to avoid unrelated things in the diff.
@ashley-cui Is the above sufficient to get the matching container-libs PR merged?
@meln5674 Yep, sorry, I added another small nit, but other than that, we're waiting for another maintainer to merge the PR, as we require 2 lgtms.

@ashley-cui Understood. I made the nit'ed changes and rebased this branch on them. Once the container-libs PR is merged, I will point back common back to upstream and squash and all changes to a single commit for final review and CI run.

It doesn't look like container-libs has been assigned a new semantic version. I'll go ahead and re-vendor on the commit hash for now, but should it be like that for the final merge? Should there be a separate PR that just bumps the dependency?

It also appears that the CI is complaining about differences in tool versions, which I suspect are between HEAD on container-libs and podman.

EDIT: Didn't realize there was a special Makefile target for vendoring, going to try that. For whatever reason that wasn't needed for vendoring the fork.

Not sure if this is a me problem, but github has been stuck on "Checking for the ability to merge automatically..." for the last hour and change, and [hasn't detected](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/24601/commits) the [new commit](https://github.com/meln5674/podman/commits/feature/remote-tls/) I pushed. Unsure of what I can do about this.
@meln5674 Strange, feel free to re-push or to close and re-open the PR, see if that kicks the CI.
Force-push is usually the most reliable way
Force push did the trick. I think the three failures are flakes again, as they don't look related to remotes, could those be checked and restarted?
Concur all look like flakes, restarted
One more WSL flake, it looks like.
re-started the flaked test.
LGTM @containers/podman-maintainers PTAL and merge

> It doesn't look like container-libs has been assigned a new semantic version. I'll go ahead and re-vendor on the commit hash for now, but should it be like that for the final merge? Should there be a separate PR that just bumps the dependency?

To add some context we update by commit during the dev cycle, only before we cut new podman/buildah releases we cut tags on the other modules and then use those. Otherwise we would end up with a ton of new tags during dev which doesn't seem that helpful.
